description:
(from publisher's website)

The Iranian push toward the Azeri capital of Baku has to go thru the border city of Salyan. As the Azeris and Iranians battle it out, the Russians and Americans both make cameos. Lots of open tank country surrounding a city that hugs a river, and makes for some challenging maneuver

Asian Thunder covers the opening battles of a hypothetical war between Iran and Azerbaijan.  On their march north, the Iranians must subdue the border city of Salyan, but the Azeris are defending on their home turf.

A challenging map has a swath or urban terrain along the river, with wide-open flanks.  Forces include Iranian and Azeri infantry, armor, recon, and attack helicopters.  Local insurgents, Russian paratroopers, and a company of American infantry also make appearances.

The Warfighter series brings modern maneuver combat to the tabletop at the tactical level.  Units are platoons and sections, with some individual vehicles and leaders. Counters are a robust 7/8" square, with values for firepower, armor, range, and movement, plus the occasional frontal armor of zone of control.  Maps are designed to correspond to US military maps, replacing 1000m grid squares with large hexes.  Scenarios are written as 5-paragraph-style Operations Orders, which are kept under wraps so neither player knows his opponents' exact mission.

boardgame: Orange Crush: Civil War in the Ukraine (Future Conflict Series)
description:
(from publishers' website)

The Next Wars series brings battalion-level modern shooting wars to your tabletop. Combat units must synchronize attacks and defenses to maximize their collective combat power. Headquarters units coordinate actions on the battlefield and support battalions maintain supply lines. 
The Next Wars is scaled at 10km/hex, with 12-hour turns. Units are battalions, headquarters, and specialty companies.

Following a series of contentious elections in which both sides accused the other of support from outside the country, the Ukraine began to fracture.   What started as competing protest marches in the streets rapidly escalated into a shooting war between the different factions.   When the President of the Ukraine finally ordered the Army to restore order, several units revolted, and the President appealed to NATO for assistance. Ignoring Russian warnings against intervening, NATO provided a small UK-led force, which the Russians countered with a reinforced mechanized corps, plus reinforcements from their Belorussian allies.   The US sent their available forces to the Polish frontier, hoping that their deterrent effect would stabilize the situation. The Ukrainian "Interventionists" (so named for their favorability toward Russian "intervention") had organized their own fighting force around the two mechanized brigades (and assorted smaller units) that mutinied against the national command.   Russian operatives assisted in arming and organizing the "101 Brigade" from provinces near the border; other partisans throughout the Ukraine also took up arms on the Interventionist side.

The Ukrainian government incorporated their volunteers into the standing army, hoping to avoid any public relations backlash from having irregular forces on the battlefield, as they attempted to paint the conflict as a civil war in which the Russians were meddling and NATO were invited peacekeepers. The first battles were joined near Lvov, as the Interventionists bypassed Kiev and pushed as far west as possible, hoping to prevent the NATO forces from establishing a bridgehead in the Ukraine. Russian and Belorussian reinforcements arrived from the north to try and flank the existing Ukrainian national forces before NATO could join the fight. The Americans were moving through Poland, but had concerns about the security of their supply lines.

Civilians are a fact of life on the modern battlefield, and they are present in The Next Wars. Neutral and benign, local civilians may come to support one faction or another based on the behavior of the forces of the battlefield. Headquarters units also enable players to deploy command cards that allow give units special actions or bonuses on the battlefield. Simulating the staff planning process, the mission cards allow units to prepare and rehearse certain missions for later execution.

boardgame: Warfare in the Ancient World: Wargames Rules for the Period 3000 B.C. to 500 A.D.
description:
Subtitled: wargames rules for the period 3000 BC to 500 AD

Miniatures rules in a 65 page A4 rules book with pull out quick reference sheet.

Troops classified for equipment, armour, training, morale and basic type.   Includes classifications for prominent troop types from many armies in the back of the rules. Armies are split up into divisions.

Combat uses factors for troop type and situation and charts to calculate losses.  Movement is according to pre-written orders.

Includes sections dealing with sieges and naval operations.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups
family: Players: Wargames with Rules Supporting Only Two Players

description:
Players move around a Monopoly-like board in which each space represents a state, fund raising and spending the money to open campaign offices.  Each office on a state increases the chances of collecting votes during primaries which are held on a fixed calendar at certain turns of the game.  Movement is 0-5 squares at player's choice, but an end-of-turn die role introduces random events.

Strategy consists primarily of shrewd choice of states in which to compete -- you can spread over many small states, concentrate on a few large ones, or make some attempt at a hybrid.  Player vs. player interaction is limited; the end-of-turn event roll gives you a 1/6th chance of being able to send an opponent to the Federal Election Commission space (which is, effectively, jail).
 Components, unusually, include a cork board and colored pins. The game otherwise has poor production values by modern standards (it appears to have been a self-published one-shot and is more than 25 years old), but is redeemed by a simple and effective design.

This is a quick-playing, light-weight, clever game that exploits its topic well and is worth two or three plays in the unlikely event that you can find a copy.

It is regrettable that the game does not credit its designer, and the name of the publisher is an obvious joke punning on "gerrymander"

This game is long out of print and rare.

boardgame: The World Cup Game:  Expansion Set 3
description:
The World Cup Game - Expansion 3 was released in February 2008 and includes four more boards to add another angle to your World Cup experience. 
This expansion set concentrates on two of the stranger formats for the World Cup. Brazil 1950 has 13 teams divided 'very' unequally due to the refusal of some teams who qualified, to turn up for the event! It was also the only World Cup to be decided on a Final Pool format.
Mexico 1986 and Italy 1990 included 24 teams from which 16 progressed to the next stage - the Round of 16. Out of the 6 groups of 4, even the 4 best third place teams progressed! Both of these games mean that it is unlikely that anyone will be eliminated in the first round. The rules include the table which determines which third place teams play which group winners!
Also included is a Euro 2008 game which allows players to choose their own team rankings. The designer has put his reputation on the line with pre-determined rankings to use if you don't want to create your own.

Expands:

    The World Cup Game

102,000 years ago (2000 years before the Martian civilization reached its peak) the Venusians crushed the Martians early pyramids with their superior polyhedron shape. Today the only thing that remains of the Venusians is this game...

Players setup the pyramids in a cross formation touching each other, centered on an overturned mouse pad and role a D20 at the pyramids. Scoring more points for knocking down more pyramids. Players get additional balls for throwing strikes.

Game System

    Icehouse Pieces

Players sit at opposite sides of the board. The pieces are oriented to point away from a player to show that that player controls them. The remaining pyramids are placed in a common area off the board that is available to both players. To show that neither player owns any of these pyramids initially, they should be in this "global stash" standing upright. There are also two initially empty off-board "prisoner areas", one near each player, to hold the pieces captured by that player.  The object of the game is to capture all of an opponent's Monarchs (large blue pieces). There are twelve different pieces in the game, each with a unique movement.

Design Competitions

    Ice Game Design Competition - Entry in 1st Competition

Moscow Ice is an abstract strategy game similar to Chinese checkers. To play you will need a different coloured Icehouse stash for each player  and a (modified) 8×8 Chessboard. [The game is played on a central 6×6 grid with each side extended by a centrally placed single row of 4 – this is an 8x8 Chessboard minus three Squares in each corner.

Links to rules for both the original Moscow Ice and the game creator's variants (Mega-Multi Ice!, Multi-Moscow Ice, and Maxi-Moscow Ice!) are available HERE.

On each turn a player will place a single piece in a space on the board, lying down and pointing to an adjacent space (or off the board). If there is another piece in the space pointed to, that piece is rotated one notch clockwise (i.e. 90 degrees on a square grid, 60 degrees on a hex grid.) If the pieces just rotated now points to a space that contains a piece, that piece is also rotated one notch clockwise, and if that piece now points to a space which contains a piece… This process is continued until a piece points to an empty space or off the edge of the board. Then play passes to the next player to the left.

Rules Link

Game System

    Icehouse Pieces


Design Competitions

    Ice Game Design Competition - Entry in 1st Competition

Each player secretly chooses 24 “Missiles” (pyramids), eight of each size, and combines them into a Battery of eight “Missile Silos” (stacks of pyramids).

Each player also secretly conceals their President and Vice President tokens under one or two of the Missile Silos.

Players take turns launching one Missile (removing one pyramid from one stack) at one specific exposed Missile in an enemy Battery. Both pyramids are discarded exposing whatever is underneath.

Eventually, the Silo protecting the President is exhausted and the President herself is exposed. Once exposed, the President can be struck by an enemy Missile, eliminating the owning player.

Play continues until only one President remains (one player winner) – OR – all remaining Presidents are exposed at the same time (a draw).

Game System

    Icehouse Pieces


Design Competitions

    Ice Game Design Competition - Entry in 1st Competition

Hunt is a game for 2-4 players hunting each other across a moving play field of a deadly nature using any two Treehouse sets. Players move large and medium pyramids around the board, creating a maze, while trying to keep their stack of small pyramids alive.

Rules Link

Game System

    Icehouse Pieces


Design Competitions

    Ice Game Design Competition - Entry in 7th Competition

Draw a card and read it out loud to your friends. Do you think the answer is totally YES? Do your friends say NO way, no how? Vote for what you think the correct answer is and see what Raven had to say about it. If your answer matches Raven's final answer, collect a gem.

The first player to place 5 gems on the gameboard is the winner Will it be you? Just Ask Raven!

Contents: Raven's crystal ball game unit, 46 question cards, 24 fortune disks, 20 plastic tokens, 4 Ask Again cards, 4 Yes/No chips, gameboard, instructions.

boardgame: Storm of Arrows: Late Medieval Europe at War – Field of Glory Gaming Companion
description:
The second army supplement for the Field of Glory: Ancient and Medieval Wargaming Rules miniatures wargame. This supplement covers the armies of late medieval Europe. There are 26 separate lists, each with a sample starter army and point cost chart for making your own force.

The book itself is 88 pages printed in full color with photos of miniatures and paintings from various other Osprey books used throughout.

Armies included:
- Hundred Years' War English (Continental)
- Hundred Years' War English (Britain)
- Wars of the Roses English
- Medieval Welsh
- Later Medieval Scots (Britain)
- Later Medieval Scots (Continental)
- Later Scots Isles and Highlanders
- Later Anglo-Irish
- Medieval Irish
- Medieval French
- Ordonnance French
- Free Company
- Navarrese
- Later Low Countries
- Medieval Burgundian
- Ordonnance Burgundian
- Swiss
- Later Medieval German
- Later Medieval Danish
- Later Medieval Swedish
- Condotta Italian
- Medieval Crown of Aragon
- Medieval Portugese
- Medieval Castilian
- Santa Hemandad Nueva Castilian

From back of the book:

In peace prepare for war.

Fight with brave knights, stalwart men-at-arms and deadly archers in the epic battles of the late medieval era with Storm of Arrows, the Field of Glory gaming comapnion.  Command noble and commoner alike as they struggle in armour, mud, and blood across the battlefields of Europe, with lance, sword, pike, and bow.  Complete with organization charts for hundreds of accurately researched troop types, detailed historical overview and supporting maps and artwork, this addition to the core rulebook will dramatically enhance your gameplay.

Storm of Arrows is a companion to Field of Glory, the historical miniatures tabletop wargaming rules system for ancient and medieval wargaming.  To play the rules you will need a copy of Field of Glory.

description:
Scrabble The Shrek Edition is a themed Scrabble game around DreamWorks's Shrek series. The game features various characters from the Shrek series on the board as bonus zones.

The game is unique from regular Scrabble by the way it is shaped. Instead of being square, the board is shaped around Shrek's head, including a rounded bottom and top, with two rounded antenna by the sides of his head. There is also special rules that give bonuses for spelling words related to the Shrek series such as "Donkey," "Fiona," and "Shrek."
family: Folk Tales & Fairy Tales: Puss in Boots

description:
This game utilizes a hybrid card-driven/area-impulse system. The game covers the Battles of Yamazaki and Shizugatake in 1582 and 1583 respectively, in which Toyotomi Hideyoshi's victories established his position as successor to Oda Nobunaga.

The game comes with two separate A2-sized maps for the two separate battles, 198 counters and 32 cards.

Pink Panther is being held in a zoo, guarded by several zoo guards. Scattered around the zoo are eight face-down tool cards (crowbar, ladder, lifebuoy etc.) which are needed to release the Pink Panther, depending on what cage he's in. Moving around by the die roll, players must find the right tool and release Pink Panther from the cage, at which point they will start playing as the Pink Panther, trying to escape from the zoo. If a player lands on a zoo guard while carrying a tool they have been spotted and must replace the tool on the board; if they land on a guard as the Pink Panther, the guard puts him into the nearest cage, and he must be released again.

The player who manages to escape from the zoo with the Pink Panther wins.

There's also a rules variant where the game is played over several rounds in teams of two players.

This is Ravensburger game no. 00 147 7 (601 5 492 2)

A multi-player Three Kingdoms game where players take on the persona of famous heroes of the Three Kingdoms history and have secret victory objectives to achieve.

198 counters
Rulebook

description:
This is the classic game of Checkers but it includes 3 enhanced gameplay options where kids can use special abilities attributed to various pieces associated with the Cars characters.

There are 4 ways to play:
Normal checkers - Plays with standard checker rules.
King Conflict - When a checker is "kinged", flip it over to reveal its special Cars skill.
Power Challenge - A head-to-head challenge where all the Cars characters are revealed and you must decide when to use their special skills.
Ultimate Race - You know which spaces your Cars characters are hidden on, but does your opponent?

Each side has 7 pieces with stickers of different Cars characters on them, normally hidden from view by a flip-up "hood ornament mover" mechanism, along with a number of plain checkers pieces without stickers.  The Cars characters represented include:
Lightning McQueen (Turbo Jump power)
Sally (Caution Cone power)
Guido (Repair power)
Mater (Tow Rope power)
King (Speed power)
Doc (Drafting power)
Luigi (Fender Rub power)

UBOS (Ultimate Book of Spells)

Based in the children's animated television series which began production in 2001.

A game of magic and enchantments. In the center of the Earth are Cassy, Gus and Verne with UBOS. If you come there first you will be able to share with them infinite adventures, floods of magic and enchantments, in their fight against the Supreme Wizard Zarlak and his perverse followers.

The game objective is to be the first one putting both player pieces in the center of the board, in UBOS's place, where Cassy, Gus and Verne are waiting.

Content:

- 1 Board
- 1 Instructions card for the places
- 8 player pieces in 4 different colors
- 2 dices

Millions of words, 100 categories and only 26 letters make Link 26 one of the most challenging new games of the year! Players must quickly think of words that link together using the letter drawn for a specific category.

For example, the category is "Things That Make Music." The letter "F" is drawn. Player A says "Flute". Now player B must use the last letter in that word to come up with the next thing that makes music... how about Euphonium?

Can be played as a solitaire game or with 2 or more players.

Game is advertized as the Wildest, Wackiest most Wonderful Magnetic Chase Game Yet. Its a game of cops and robbers. Its trying to keep the robber from getting the money or trying to catch the robbber or trying to keep the robber from catching you.

From the box top:
"Be the first to capture your assigned spy and link up with the orbiting Justice League Satellite!"

Each player plays a Wonder Woman. This allows for a bit of player interaction. When you land a spot occupied by another Wonder Woman you battle it out. Why would Wonder Woman fight herself? I have no idea.

The idea is to find words for certain categories whose initials are drawn. The referee gives his OK for the best word with a hammer. The pawns are moved forwards after a word has won. The first player with his pawn at the finish wins the game.

From the publisher's website:

Zoom through the jungle at breakneck speed!

Excitement in the jungle. It’s market day and the jungle inhabitants want to sell their fruit. A large troop of monkeys helps them to carry the heavy baskets to market. But watch out - thieves have their eye on all the goodies. The other jungle inhabitants want to steal your fruit. Along with Pongo, the little monkey, they take the nicest fruit out of your baskets! Pongo is lurking somewhere along the long trek through the jungle and suddenly jumps out of the trees – in one fell swoop, your fruit is all gone. And what about you? If you get a chance, you can also try, with Pongo’s help, to steal fruit out of another basket. That’s the way of the jungle. Just take care that you don’t take a coconut by mistake. No-one likes them and you’ll have to keep them. The winner is the person who has brought the most fruit to market. So off you go!
contents

1 gameboard (11 ½ x 11 ½ in./29 x 29 cm), 12 baskets, 1 magnetised monkey, palm tree structures, 32 fruit chips, 2 palm tree tops, 3 market places, 2 coloured dice, 25 action cards, instructions

From the publisher's website:

Ingenious square combinations for far-sighted players.

You need an eye here for what’s important. The winner is the person who forms the most squares with his pegs. It sounds easier than it actually is because you so often overlook the most obvious solution. The game for people with strategic skills and a mind for an unusual eye view!
contents

One gameboard (11 ¾ x 11 ¾ in./30 x 30 cm), 48 playing pegs, 28 winning points (wooden chips), large print instructions

An easy game for children to play. Each player has a side on the board that shows the Farmer, his wife, child, nurse, dog, cat, rat and cheese. Spin the spinner and match the correct pictures in order. Game pieces are used to cover each picture of the Farmer, wife, child, ect...as they go. The winner is the one who is first to spin and get the cheese.

There are 4 game rules included in each deck.

This game was developed for two purposes. It was developed with young children and their parents in mind. All age groups can play the same game and experience challenges appropriate to their age. Secondly, we chose birds because they are  important to our ecosystem and have significant habitat overlap with humans. We wanted to create a game where children and adults would learn about the natural world around them. As a result of that learning, we hoped people would go out to explore and protect their natural world.

More games rules are available for free download. Games include basic memory games for children, and more advanced set building and area control games for adults.

boardgame: Star Wars: Mos Eisley Adventure Set
description:
From the back of the box:

This desert spaceport city is the ultimate adventuring locale for fans of Star Wars movies.  This boxed set is a guide to the hazard and opportunities to be found in Mos Eisley, with numerous adventures, characters and locations for players to explore.

The game contains 12 metal miniatures.  A 32 page adventure book, a Mos Eisley galaxy guide, a two sided map and dice.

boardgame: Lowell Thomas Travel Game: World Cruise
description:
From the rules: "The object of Lowell Thomas' World Cruise game is to circle the globe and return to New York first.

The game includes, as an important part of the play, educational features which teach the players the main points of interest in the many cities and lands which they visit in the course of their travels."

A roll-and-move race game with a bit of money management, different modes of transportation, and trivia questions.  Mule and sailing vessel (for $100), bicycle and tramp steamer ($400), train and cruise ship ($700), and airplane and zeppelin ($1000).  Note that trans-continental zeppelin travel was a real possibility when the game was designed, just before the Hindenburg disaster in 1937.  Each mode gives you a multiplier on your die roll (times 1 through times 4) for movement.  Two decks of cards (blue ones for random benefits and red ones for random penalties) correspond to blue and red spaces on the board.  Don't just go to jail, go to Devil's Island or the fortification of Tokio (sp).  Numbered spaces offer the chance to answer trivia questions in two books (one for Globe Trotters, one for Young People) to win or lose $100.

"Slay all of the wizards’ beasts and get Thrag home in time for breakfast."

The red, green and blue tiles are the beasts summoned by the three evil wizards. Thrag must defeat all fifteen of these nasties in twelve hours (turns) or the game is lost. Higher numbers on a tile reflect a more challenging foe.

Track turns by moving the black pawn clockwise around the outer spaces of the 2×2 grid. Twelve turns have elapsed when the pawn returns to the upper-left space. The red, green and blue coins can help Thrag battle against the beasts of a particular wizard.

When Thrag fights a blue foe, he can only flip a blue coin, and so on. The pawns of these colors represent three special weapons at Thrag’s disposal.

The dice are used to randomize strengths on both sides of the battle. Aces count as 1s, Nulls count as 0s.

The black coins track Thrag’s hit points. At game start, three of the coins are in use while the other three are in a face-up Healing Pool. The black tiles represent chances for Thrag to draw coins from the pool and increase his current hit-point total.

Game System

    Piecepack Game System

Your goal is to have the longest, highest value tract of land because the player with the highest score wins. Players draw tokens from a bag to determine where they are allowed to play their pyramids and attempt to take control of a tract of their neighbor's land.

Game System

    Piecepack Game System
    Icehouse Game System

A game for 2-4 players
Ages 8 and up
Game Time: 20-45 minutes

Welcome to the Temple of Favors.  You and your fellow players are priests in ancient Ayzactal, attempting to complete your final test, your most difficult test: obtaining the Favor Masks.  The rooms are tight, the doorways are narrow, and rumors increase of a strange curse lurking in the dark corners of the temple.

In TEMPLE, players scramble through the ancient temple, trying to gather the favor masks, all while trying to keep their opponents from doing the same.  The first player to obtain three Favor Masks wins the game.

TEMPLE blends hand management, area control, and set collection with a little touch of "take that."

Contents:
60 Temple Cards
4 Favor Mask Cards
4 Wooden Player Markers
4 Wooden Curse Markers
1 Cloth Temple Play Mat
Rules

boardgame: Apples to Apples Junior: Jewish Edition
description:
This is a version of Apples to Apples designed for kids, with cards optimized for middle-school aged children (Ages 9 and up).  The subject material of the cards focuses on Jewish history, religion, and culture.

The game consists of two decks of cards: Things and Descriptions. Each turn, the current referee selects a Description and players try to pick, from the cards in their hands, the Things that best match that Description. The referee then chooses the Thing that appeals most and awards the card to the player who played it. The unusual combinations of Things and Descriptions are humorous to the extreme, and will quickly have the entire room in an uproar. Once a player has won a pre-determined number of cards, that player wins.

This is a party game with simplified mechanics from the original version, DICEcapades.  This version comes with far fewer dice than the original version (13 vs. 133).  Players navigate a board while competing in several activities, including trivia questions, pictionary-style drawing, word and number play, and physical challenges.

Contents: 13 jumbo dice, 20 Actionland cards, 20 Thinkingtown cards, 20 Triviaville cards, writing pad, pencil, 4 character pawns, sand timer, instructions. Ages 6 and up.

description: This game is a re-creation of prairie life in South Dakota.  Each player chooses an animal from one of five groups (Waterfowl, Upland Game, Small Mammal, Big Game, and Predator) and tries to make it around the board, facing hazards along the way.  After they have completed a lap, they switch to an animal from another group and go again, until they have completed the game with all five animal groups.

The Appreciation Game

The object of the game is to allow family and friends to become better acquainted and to be aware of and appreciate their own uniqueness while sharing together

Point is thinkably simple. The basis for this game is the classical Pachisi for which Nuernberger has put together some exciting new versions.
In spite of using dice POINT is not a game of pure chance. The dice bring in some surprising effects. You win with your own head.

This game board is like a kind of flipper, where the players play in their chips, in order to get out some more for themselves.

At the beginning of the game, a chip is placed on each of the three corner spaces of the game board. All other chips are equally distributed to the players. Eventually leftover chips are put on the last space marked with an arrow.

The players roll a die and set a chip on the corresponding space on the game board, 1 beeing the center space. If there is already one or more chips on the space, the player rolls again and moves the entire stack accordingly.
What a player moves over the arrow field onto the table are his winnings.

GIN is game No. 10 (of a least 12) in a family called "Miniplays".

Content: board, 2 x 3 big dice, 6 wooden chips

Two players try to decide for themselves in as many rounds as possible how to get the highest scores  possible out of the rolled dice. With a cute rule addition and 6 chips the game will be an exciting race.

This is game No. 3 (of at least 12) in a family called "Miniplays"

Content: board, 3 big dice, 2 x 6 wooden chips

On there turn each player tries to get  as high a score as possible by a mixture of the luck of the dice and his/her own decisions.

MIX is No. 7 (of at least 12) games in a family called "Miniplays".

Content: board out of leather, 2 dice, 4 x 4 jewels

Each player tries to move his 4 stones from his/her own quarter crisscross to the opposite quarter. There are a lot of possibilities. Therefore, not only is luck necessary, but also skill and courage while trying to take a risk.

Content: 3 white dice, 3 black dice, 1 red die

2 players try to roll as many high score rounds as possible. The player with the highest total wins.

Game No. 1 (of at least 12) in the family called "Miniplays".

The sliding cover of the game box contains a number band with characters 1 - 10. With this piece the players can measure reactions of other players in the game. One player holds the number band by the 10 and lets it fall while another player tries to catch it as fast as possible. The number where you caught the wooden piece is the number of small wooden sticks you get for the construction of a playing field. The player with the best reaction wins the game.

The aim of the game is to bring as many of your own round stones as possible on to the topmost platform of the pyramid. The game is over as soon as 5 stones are on this platform. The player with the most pawns on top wins the game.

The aim of the game is to move as many pawns as possible to the opposite corner. The player who has all his still in the game remaining pawns in the opposite corner wins. Even if one player has left only one pawn on the board, he wins the game by having the one pawn in the opposite corner.

With 4 dice the 20 soccer pawns are moved over the board. When a pawns is moved over the basic line of the opponent a goal is shot.
The player with the most goals wins the game.

The object of the game is to get rid of the chips on the board with good dice combinations by trying to make as many points as possible.#

This is game No. 5 (of at least 12) in a family called "Miniplays".

There are 5 cards each representing a person in the community (teacher, firefighter, etc) - one card is a picture of that person - the other 4 match the person. Deal out person cards to all players. Place the rest of the cards face-up randomly on the table. One, two, three go - all players at once must grab the object cards that belong to their person. Whoever grabs all 4 first rings the bell. There are different ways to play this game, to be enjoyed by a wide age-range of players. Players: 2 to 4. Ages 4 and up

The Educa Salent SA version includes 3 different games to play.

Contents: 56 object cards, 14 "career" cards, 1 bell

boardgame: The Play's The Thing Comedy Set
description:
An expansion set to the game: The Play's the Thing: Game of  Shakespearean Theater.

The set features cards from the plays: As You Like It, Twelfth Night, and A Midsummer Night's Dream.

Each set allows players to dash and duel around the Globe Theater board, while bringing the characters, the words and the plots of Shakespeare to life in a fun, engaging way.

Each of the three card sets inside this expansion set includes: 
Character Cards -  with questions and facts about the memorable characters of each play. 
Script and Quote Cards - that bring context and meaning to Shakespeare's words. 
Plot Cards -  that help you understand Shakespeare, instead of just reading it. 
Cast Card - that identifies the major players of each featured play.

Expands:

    The Play's the Thing

The pressure's on as student surgeons try to assemble all the patient's organs—without spilling its guts! Using the included 100 true/false question cards, students answer questions about the human body and its organ systems. When they answer correctly, they earn an organ to place in the body tray. When they answer incorrectly, they spill the guts—and start over! Collect the most correctly answered question cards to win. Game features 11 plastic organ pieces: heart, 2 lungs, 2 kidneys, diaphragm with trachea, pancreas with spleen, stomach, liver, small and large intestines and rib cage. Clear plastic body tray measures approximately 16"L. For 2–4 players or teams.

Chuz-It allows players to choose what they want to do on their turn. With the Flashback process, players get to show off their skills in topics such as Math, Spelling and Geography as well as having fun making up words on a Word Jumble, singing lines from songs in Sing It, making up stories in Story Teller, seeing what you can draw in Blind Art, or trying not to get twisted up on a Tongue Twister and more! Board advancement (or the ability to send others backwards) is based on the difficulty of the chosen talent challenge. Contents of Chuz-It:game board, 240 playing cards, Doodle pad, Pencil, 4 playing pawns, 30 second timer, Instructions.  2 or more or teams, Ages 7 and up

Trick or treat with Halloween Jenga. Build your tower with orange and black blocks. As the tower builds so does the difficulty. Custom Halloween Jenga rules and a special glow-in-the-dark die make the game even more challenging and fun!Roll the die and you are in for a trick... or treat. Roll a "Pumpkin" and reverse the direction of play; roll a "Haunted House" and remove any piece or roll a "Bat" and remove only an orange block. The last player to remove a block without tumbling the tower wins the game. Game comes complete with 54 Halloween-themed hard wood Jenga blocks, 1 custom glow-in-the-dark die, custom Halloween rules and a loading tray for assembly.

MLB-branded trivia game which features more than 800 up-to-date questions about all 30 teams and their players, complete with team logos and graphics. Most questions are multiple choice or true/false, so players don't need to be an expert to play. Gameplay is much like a real baseball game (hits, runs, outs, etc.) Contents include game board, 4 batting figures, 4 running figures, spinner, scoring pad and rules.

Educational children's game which teaches the food pyramid and nutritional categories of food.  Players spin a spinner to get a category of food (such as "dairy" or "grains").  Players then locate an image of a food on their card that fits into that nutritional category.  The first player to make a row of matches on their card wins.

Preschooler game where players spin, draw cards, and then compete to find the cookie piece with the correct, matching shape.  The spinner and game cards have no writing, so no reading is necessary.  The cookie pieces are nice plastic pieces that resemble actual cookies in various shapes with various decorations.

Educational game for children to teach about the USDA's new MyPyramid Food Guidance System and promotes physical fitness. Players spin and move, answer questions, and try to be the first to complete their pyramid.  Includes 6 double-sided food pyramid game boards, 2 double-sided spinners, 2 die and 140 game cards. For 2-6 players.

boardgame: Pearly Whites: A Dental Health Game
description:
Educational children game using the theme of dental health.  The gameboard is a large, open mouth, and players move their pawns along the path of the teeth in the mouth.  
Players spin, move, and answer true/false trivia questions about good oral hygiene in order to win.

From the publisher: "Take a bit out of dental visits with this game that's all about your choppers. Learn the names of teeth by moving around the mouth-shaped game board in Level 1 play. Answer true/false questions in Level 2 play to brush up on proper oral hygiene. Dental floss, mouth wash, toothpaste and toothbrush pawns offer another reason to smile. Also includes game board, spinner, 100 true/false question cards and guide."

Players spin and move around a board trying to collect word cards to put together a grammatically correct sentence.  Uses a theme of chefs trying to "cook" up correct sentences using recipes.

From the publisher: "Proper grammar is easy as pie when "chefs" follow recipes to make complete sentences by gathering and correctly identifying parts of speech. Draw a free ingredient card and add the funny word of your choice to the mix. Read complete sentences aloud for giggles and spoken grammar practice. Parts of Speech Cards feature color-coded backs for self-checking."

Special-edition Monopoly board features famous Lakers players throughout history. Also contains six collectible Lakers-themed pewter tokens, including: Lakers Logo, Basketball Hoop, NBA Basketball, NBA Championship Trophy, Defensive Player and Offensive Player.

Children's dexterity game themed on the popular Sesame Street character.  Players compete to place plastic "feathers" into holes in the game board (depicting Elmo) and trying to not to "tickle" him, setting of a laughing alarm sound. Ages 3 - 5

This is a crossword-style game for children Ages 6 - 11.  Players build words from letters rolled on dice.  The dice are hollow, however, and are intended to contain small prizes rewarding players for having made words.

From the publisher: "Wonder Letters is the first crossword game where players create words and open WonderLetters to claim hidden prizes. Prizes are customizable - they can be tricks or treats, ranging from motivational stickers or jelly beans to jumping jacks! WonderLetters rounds out the fun with its unique triple-play rule and fast-paced, all-play piggyback round."

Wonder Checkers is a teaching implementation of Checkers. In addition to the regular checker board, the individual pieces are shaped like small cannisters, which open to reveal messages and prizes. The kit comes with stickers, customizable inserts, prize-tracking score sheets and game rules in quite a readable, logical sequence for new learners.

While the prizes supplied with the game are mere stickers and tokens, the designer suggests using jelly beans!

Wonder Checkers is part of a series which includes Wonder Chess, Wonder Go, and Wonder Letters, all of which are designed to teach standard rules and strategy of these classic abstract games by inserting intermediate rewards and tracking tools to motivate young learners.

On the  reverse side of the board, an implementation of 3-d tic-tac-toe is also included.

Recommended for Ages 4 and up

Players answer questions related to the Bible to move up a rainbow gameboard. There are 2100 questions that cover seven categories (corresponding to the seven colors of the rainbow): Old Testament; History and Geography; Prophets and Miracles; Names; Letters, Numbers, and Sequence; New Testament; and Wisdom. The first player to successfully move up the rainbow and answer an additional question from a category chosen by the other players wins.

The questions are designed for children, Ages 6 and up.

boardgame: This is your town: Parramatta game
description:
The object of the game is to move around the Australian city of Parramatta and acquire ten destinations cards by moving around the gameboard. The first player to do so, wins.

The contents of the game box reveal a mounted mapboard showing the cbd (central business district) of Parramatta divided into movement spaces, a pair of six-sided dice, 12 player tokens, a pack of money in different denominations, a pack of surprise cards and a pack of destination cards.

In preparation, each player is given a $100 cash scrip and a token. Tokens are placed in the locations indicated on the board, as are the shuffled surprise and destination cards. The first destination card is turned face up.

Each player takes a turn by rolling the two dice and moving round the board with the object of being first to reach the destination address. A player rolling doubles is allowed to go directly to the destination and claim the card. Each destination card claimed requires a payment of $50. Extra money may be acquired by moving to indicated spaces at a rate of ten times a dice roll (doubled if doubles  are rolled). Spaces marked 'surprise' require a relevant card to be taken by a player and the text is acted upon. A player landing upon another's space may purchase a destination card for $25 from that player or take another turn.

The game ends when one player has collected ten destination cards.

Players are famous tank manufacturers of World War II, such as Krupp and Vickers, aiming to build the best tank of WWII.

Game comes with 108 "Magic: The Gathering"-sized cards.

This game is the third in the series from the same game designer, after Naval Competition and Prototype Competition.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
This is a multi-player card game in which players take part in the naval arms race in the run-up to World War I, aiming to build the best battleship and prepare for the upcoming conflict.

The game comes with 108 cards.

The designer also designed similar games subsequent to this: Tank Extravaganza and Prototype Competition.

Published in old Game Journal #61.

This is a card game. Players are World War II aircraft manufacturers trying to design the best fighter plane of WWII. They can literally put together their dream plane, from jet-engined Zeros to Mustangs bristling with 50mm cannons.

The game comes with 117 cards.

This game is a spin-off from Tank Extravaganza that was published in Game Journal #61, also from the same game designer. The designer also subsequently designed a similar game called Naval Competition.

Published in old Game Journal #64.

From the covers:

Sure...NASA is sending missions to Mars...
...but we're preparing to go...
Exploring the Depths of Uranus!

A mining game of strategy and resource management set at the very edge of the solar system.

Exploring the Depths of Uranus is a planning and resource strategy game where every decision you make will leave profound consequences. Trade, explore, or just shoot anything that moves...the choices are yours to make. Find priceless artifacts, valuable ores, and friendly (or possibly hostile) natives, but remember: this is Uranus, a harsh environment filled with toxic gases and unpleasant surprises.

This is the classic game of Monopoly with an America's National Parks theme.  It uses images and names of 22 of America's National Parks, and has appropriately themed pewter tokens: Fishing Reel, Mountain Bike, Camera, Tent, Hiking Boot, and Canoe.
A simple math children's game. Players place cards on table and make equations. The aim is to get rid all of your cards.

Players impersonate squirrels who are looking for food: fungi, nuts, pine cones, rowan-berries. Cards are put in one pile and revealed one by one while forming another pile. When someone uncovers a squirrel then he/she must say which kind of food his animal eats. Player takes every card in reject pile with food that he has chosen and lays those cards next to others with this food. The player who collects the most number of cards wins the game.
Game contents 54 cards. 30 with palm trees, 15 with monkeys and 9 with bananas. Players collect cards with bananas.

Mare Bella is a children's game that takes place at the sea side. Players have to collect 5 hidden types of sea animals and avoid picking up rubbish such as tires and old boots that have been washed up by the tide. But if they do, they will have the opportunity to rid the beach of such litter, which brings its own reward.

For the younger player, the game has a simple environmental message; for the older player it has a gameplay in which a certain amount of skill and memory is involved.

Basic Rules:

Each player has a 'lotto' board, with space for 5 sea animals: a fish, starfish, crab, sea urchin (type thing) and an octopus. The aim is to be the first to collect all 5 of these, from amongst the square tiles that are laid out over the playing area. Players receive the 'beach bag' from which they draw a round token (same idea as dipping into the Scrabble bag that holds the letters). This token has on it a particular fish type or a particular colour on it. The player then chooses a square tile from the playing area whose upside corresponds in colour or fish type to the round token. He/she flips over the square tile to reveal its underside (which is likely to be different). If the underside is a fish that he/she hasn't collected yet, then the player adds it to his 'lotto' board. If the underside shows a pollution, he/she must collect the pollution. Once a player has three, then he/she may remove them from the game and get a 'free dip'.

Memorising the underside of certain tiles definitely helps, but is not essential, meaning that anyone can play from the age of 3 and up, although the game is certainly illustrated with kids in mind.

The game is soon to be available in the UK, costing around £18. See www.isimat.fr or email muellbauer@lycos.com for details

Tenka Fubu (War under the sky) is a multi-player game in which players take on the roles of the major samurai clans. The Takeda, Oda and Asai clans are battling for power from the 1560s to the 1570s. There's a political element, as alliances shift back-and-forth, as well as military action, as players' armies contest each other across the board.

Note: 天下布武 (Tenka Fubu) (under the sky - spread war) refers to the characters on Oda Nobunaga's personal seal that he inaugurated during this period.

Based on the TV contest Saber y Ganar (To know and To win). Ages 12 and up

3 competitors play, answering questions of general culture during diverse tests.

Content:

- 6 game pieces
- 936 questions cards
- 3 marking boards
- 1 rules book

From the publisher:

Quick, can you name a flavor of ice cream? Vanilla, chocolate, strawberry... you know tons of answers but can you be the last person to buzz in with a correct answer before the timer runs out? This game turns the concept of "sniping" a bid on eBay into the hottest new game around. With hundreds of questions and a random timer that changes every round, the fun will never stop with Snipe It! For 2 to 4 players Ages 8 and up

From the publisher:

Race against time to unscramble Rock and Roll's greatest moments! Long Live Rock is the un-trivia game that challenges you to put all of the famous people, places, music, and events on the time line of Rock and Roll. Every second counts as you arrange significant Rock happenings in chronological order. Guess the correct year of a Rock happening and go on to win. A great party game for music lovers.

For 1 or more players or teams.  Ages 14 and up

From the publisher:

"Find Puppy's Friends before the party ends!

Players take turns rolling the die and matching cards to "find" all the party guests. If you roll puppy's party, you cannot make a match and the party begins. The winner is the first to player to make 5 matches before the party ends! No reading skill required. Contents: 26 cards, die, game rules in English and French."

From the publisher:
"The fastest, highest, smallest, loudest, greatest and silliest trivia game ever! Each trivia question category - Entertainment, Food, Fun & Games, Mother Nature, Odds & Ends and People - contains 50 fascinating, far out and funny facts. Travel the game board and earn cards by answering the questions correctly. The first to collect 1 card from each category wins! The whole family can enjoy! Great for team play too. For 2-4 players or teams. Contents: game board, 4 game figures, 4 game stands, 300 trivia cards in 6 category boxes, game rules."

From the publisher: 
"The ultimate game to communicate, create, gyrate and (sometimes) frustrate! You have 60 seconds to give the best clues - can your team guess the word you're describing? Spin the spinner to determine how you give your clues. This charades, drawing and word game is fun for the entire family and comes with 250 words to guess. For 4 or more players. Contents: 250 word cards, spinner, timer, score sheet, pencils and game rules - all packed in clear bucket with lid."

From the publisher:
"Fifteen well-loved fairy tales are colorfully illustrated on game cards. Players travel the game board collecting these cards, memorizing them and placing them in their card holder, face-out so they cannot see the pictures. When you land on a "scroll" space, you must recite the cards you have collected! The first player to collect 7 fairytale cards, land on a "scroll" space and name all 7 cards correctly wins the game. No reading skills required.
Contents: game board, 4 game pawns, 4 stands to hold fairytale cards, 30 fairytale cards, game die, rules."

From the publisher: 
"Answer trivia questions about the wonderful world of horses - the various breeds, how they develop, where they live, how they're cared for, how they're ridden - each time you answer correctly you get to claim one of the 25 horses in the corral to your very own paddock. Collect the most and you win! Contents: 4 game boards (paddocks), 25 horse game figures, 25 game stands, 200 trivia cards, trivia card holder, games rules."

Contents:

     Board with spinner
     Six tokens
     Cardboard counters:
         24 gates
         18 single keys
         24 double keys
          6 triple keys
    
     Instruction booklet in five languages


Players begin with a supply of gates and keys and move clockwise on concentric paths connected at certain points via gateways.  If someone has blocked a gateway by placing a gate across it, you need one or more keys to open it, then that gate becomes yours.  You can gain more keys by moving away from the center or landing on another player.  First player to reach the center wins.

When there are three or more players, two may negotiate a contract specifying how many gates will be placed during the current turn and where.  A player involved in a contract receives a key for each gate he places as part of that contract.

Rupicola doesn't include trivia questions, but the spaces on the board are color-coded to allow players to incorporate trivia cards from Trivial Pursuit: Genus Edition (or any other such question-and-answer set) into the game.  Playing time (average 10 minutes for two players up to 60 minutes for six) can be twice as long when playing with trivia.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
From the publisher:
"Bluff your way to victory! Players take turns laying matching animal cards face down. Since you can't see them, are you sure they match? Can you trick your opponents into believing you? If you can, you can get rid of your cards very quickly. But, don't get caught bluffing or you'll get skunked! Contents: 50 cards, game rules in English and French."

Contents:

     Deck of 42 playing cards
     5 reference cards listing the cards' ranks in order
     9 information cards about the animals in the game
     Instructions


From highest to lowest the cards rank Dolphin, Manatee, Alligator, Armadillo, Killer Whale, Sea Turtle, Numbers 12 down to 1, Flamingo, Eagle, Pelican, with two cards of each rank comprising the playing deck.  Players are dealt a hand of seven cards and play out the tricks.  On your turn, you must go higher if possible.  If two like cards are played in the same trick, the second one beats the first.  The winner is simply the player who has the highest card in the last trick.

The instructions say to devise your own scoring method if desired.  They also include rules for a couple of variants in the rummy family.

Federation Commander: Briefing  provides rules updates and new scenarios for use with any of the base Federation Commander Products: Klingon Border, Romulan Border, or Academy.

Briefing #1 includes 6 new ships, two dozen new scenarios, a tactical lesson, and a complete list of all published scenarios to date.

boardgame: Fargo North, Decoder Secret Word Game
description: A 1978 The Electric Company board game. Fargo North, Decoder Secret Word Game.

In this light card game, each player is given a mushroom, which he has to feed in order for it to grow. On the menu are  fireflies, salad, trash, ...

Each mushroom has different characteristics and special powers which allow attacking other mushrooms in order to trigger indigestion...

boardgame: Zip! The Fastest Dice Game in the Universe!
description: Zip your way to the winner's circle! Shake 'em, Roll 'em, Move 'em!!! The faster you roll 'em, the faster you win. Roll your dice, keep any aside and re-roll the rest. Be the first to roll the correct combination and move. Zip is the fastest dice game in the universe. Ages 8 and up

boardgame: Conestoga: The Game of the Oregon Trail
description:
Conestoga is a game in which players can simulate the feeling of the explorers who traveled west in covered wagons.

The object of the game is for players to get from the East Coast to Oregon and make enough money to buy a ranch.  The person who buys the largest ranch wins. Events along the way will help or hinder the players achieve their goal.

From the box: "The game of scramblin' eggs!"

Twelve life-size plastic eggs, eleven containing small foam 'yolks' and the other with a foam chicken are placed on a circular battery-powered vibrating plastic tray.  In turn, players select and open one egg.

If the egg contains a yolk, the yolk is kept & the empty egg is replaced on the tray.  If the egg is empty, the player must put one of their yolks in the egg & replace it on the tray.  If the egg contains the foam chicken, the player is eliminated. All other players must yell CHICKEN OUT  & the eliminated player must return the chicken to the egg & replace it on the tray.

The first player to collect the required number of yolks (varies according to the number of players) or the last remaining player is the winner.

Content:
1 board of two parts,
4 fishermen,
4 baskets,
20 chips (things that can be drawn out of the water)
4 magnets
1 die

Family trip to the lake: Everyone is eagerly hoping to draw the best fish out of the water. There are fish, of course, 
jewel bags, secret treasure chests and unusual implements.
Who got most of the points by fishing precious things wins the game.

boardgame: Spider-Man and Friends Hide 'n Seek Card Game
description: This is a memory and matching game for preschool/early elementary age children with the theme of Spider-Man and his friends (including friendly looking Hulk and Wolverine).  Memory/matching utilizes the characters, shapes, colors, and numbers.  Different rule sets for basic and advanced versions, including the use of "clue" cards.

boardgame: That's So Raven Star of the Show Trivia
description:
From the publisher:

Show what you know about the hit TV show "That's So Raven" by answering questions in these amusing categories: That's So Raven, Psychic Eye, Is That for Real' and Who Said What' The first player to reach the end of the game path is named the Star of the Show. Interactive gameplay encourages social interaction and lots of laughter.

boardgame: That's So Raven "Tell It Like It Is" Game
description:
Social interaction/discussion style game based on the TV show.

From the publisher: "Join Raven, Chelsea, Eddie and Cory in this fun and "inquisitive" game. Who's your favorite teacher? Who's your crush? What's your favorite pizza topping? Get to know your friends by visiting their "corner" and answering questions.  2 to 4 players ages 7 and up.

Includes game mat, 32 friendship cards, 4 character tokens, 9 ch...ching tokens, psychic spinner and game rules.
Memory game in which players earn cards by feeding the third ball to each frog, while attempting to avoid overfeeding any frogs. The player with the most cards after three rounds wins.

boardgame: A Batalha do Passo do Rosário
description:
Free print and play wargame in Strategos #3. It can be downloaded from the Riachuelo website. This small game a battle between Brazilian imperial troops against Argentinian/Orientales (former Uruguay's inhabitants), in Rio Grande do Sul (state of south of the Brazil).

Features a 8.5" x 11" map of mainly very uneven terrain, 50 counters, and concise rules, terrain and combat tables in Portuguese.

Victory Conditions are determined by accumulation of Victory Points for eliminating cavalry, leaders, and artillery scored at the end of 7 turns. 
A player is awarded a Tactical Victory if by the last turn, he has scored 7 more VPs than his opponent. He wins a Decisive Victory if by the last turn, he has scored 15 more VPs than his opponent. 
Any other result is a tie.

An educational trivia game for young and expecting parents. Contains more than 300 questions related to pregnancy and the care of infants.

Each player gets a babycare card. There are 6 different categories. The player start his/her turn with throwing the die and move the pawn in the direction the player wants. The player has to answer a quetion in the category he or she lands on. If the player answers the question correctly, he/she places a Right-chip on the babycare card. Otherwise he/she places a Wrong-chip on the babycare card.
When a player has collected 6 chips, the game ends. The player with the most Right-chips, wins the game.

This is a dice game where players attempt to roll patterns of Pokémon with the dice before rolling a Poké Ball and losing their turn.

From the publisher: Chimchar, Turtwig and Piplup accompany players in this exciting new Pokémon game. Roll the dice and set aside any Pokémon character that will help you get the patterns you need on your card. Roll as many times as you want on every turn, but watch out -- if you roll the Poké Ball, your turn ends and you can't score in that round. First player to get all seven patterns wins.

This is a children's matching/counting game based on the theme of the popular children's show.  Players spin numbers on a double spinner, count to/locate pieces and look for matches in order to win "CD" pieces.  The first player to win three CD pieces.

Players follow spinner directions to try to advance along a board representing a marathon.  The first runner across the finish line wins. Multiple-choice questions quiz kids on measurement tools, length, weight, temperature and capacity, in both customary and metric scales. The marathon theme promotes fitness. Includes 125 questions cards, racer pawns, spinner and guide.

From the publisher:

At the cow's and pig's home.
Who will help the farmer and his wife to feed the animals?
If the sun is shinning the animals want to pass the time in the meadow, but if it is raining they quickly return to their stables.
Three suggestions for playing, assigning, puzzling and observing closely.
A Game & Puzzle growing with the child.
For 1 player and an adult.

Content:
board with watch hands, 20 action cards, 4 pawns, 1 wooden die

At midnight the ghosts slip out of the key holes and take their turns walking the halls. At 1 o'clock the spook ends. Which ghost will reach   a key hole? Whoever does remains visible for the next 300 years! The ghosts throw the die and move according to the roll of the die. The watch moves forward and can be moved back by throwing a 1 or 6.

Turn the disk, and change the position of the pictures! Are you able to find the pairs behind the doors? Find all five pairs one after another, and you will win the game.

This is a variant of Husker Du?, but this game requires a player to find all the matches in one turn.

Historical themed card game for 2 players depicting the Protestant break from the Catholic Church. One player is the Protestants, the other is the Catholics. Players use Faith cards to bid for control of different nations. There are separate Faith decks for each player and a shared deck of Nations. Some Faith cards have special powers that affect the Bidding. Some Faith cards have special Event powers that affect the game outside of the bidding. Faith cards have period titles such as Martin Luther, Edict of Nantes, and Council of Trent. A complete copy of the rules is available for free at:  http://www.angelfire.com/games2/warpspawn/Reform.html

Kiproko: L'extension!, an expansion for Kiproko, adds 540 new words, two sticks, one big blue pearl, and one small red one. In addition, the expansion contains three new variants to the rules:


     One for three "drawers", which allows for games with 6-12 players
     One for team play
     One for three players
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
Content:
board, 8 wooden penguins, 5 dice

Each player tries to run through a water landscape with ice floes as fast as possible and to reach the finish fields first. 2 dice give the approximate direction. The last penguin in the round may set back other further in front sitting penguins by using the ice bear. Drifting ice can change the whole game. When a player reaches one of the three finish fields with his penguin, he wins the Penguin Cup.

boardgame: N30: We Are Winning – The Battle of Seattle
description:
A game of the "Battle of Seattle" in 1999. Two players, 11x17" map, 168 counters. 13 pages of rules & 7 of notes. This is a complete redesign of N30 I and N30 II at a more detailed scale.

"N30 portrays the morning blockades of November 30th. Two players represent the opposing sides; one represents protesters; the other represents the police, with their incident commanders, Assistant Chief Ed Joiner and Captain Jim Pugel. The protesters aim to show that non-violent direct action can work, show that networked organizations can defeat hierarchical ones, and bring media attention to the issues. The police try to break the blockades and allow the conference to open as planned."

"Each turn represents 10 minutes. Each protester cluster represents 20-30 protesters, of whom 10-15 may be arrestables; each police hard or soft squad represents 10-15 police; each police mounted squad represents 6 police and each police chemical team represents 4 police (usually with 1 armored car). The police forces include most of the units of the Demonstration Management Team. They also include squads scrambled from the Convention Center venue (and/or other police units in the area)."

Six steel balls roll down a vertical board to land in various scoring bins at the bottom. boardgame: 54 Jones: Keeping up with the Joneses
description:
In 54 Jones the Baord Game, you play clones of sewer worker 54 Jones on a training day in the sewers of subterranean 12 Station City.  However, 54 Jones unleashes a tidal wave of 'shunk' to try and drown the competition.  The first clone to reach high ground wins the game.

To reach high ground, each junior Jones clone must race along the sewer network, battling his fellow clones, shirkers, Cynthia and the endless ever changing labyrinth of tunnels. Players get the chance to rotate, lock and change tiles throughout the game.  Only the most calculating and devious clone will win.

The game is now available to buy from Rathbone Games and comes with boards, counters, dice and sewer denizens.

There is a place with no time or space, wandering on the eternity of an inexistent reality. The beginning of everything and the converge of nothing. Welcome a new era of fantasy, heroism and eternal battles. The Eprion era.

In a very distant world called Eprion, two races must face each other in a great battle. The game comes with two sets of cards and figures. The cards can be creatures or spells. Each creature card has an associated figure that can be played in the board. The board is composed by 6 modular boards which must be placed at the beginning of the game: forest, ice, desert, swamp, lake and plain. The position in which they are placed is very relevant, since the creatures abilities are affected by the kind of landscape they are standing in.
The object of the game is to be the first one in defeating the adversary’s leader, which is protected by 2 gemstones and his army.

Eprion is not just a game. It comes with a very well written book with the story of this world where you can learn about the story of almost every character of the game.

This game advertises itself as "the first computer based Tank game in the world". It is a board game 'controlled' by an electronic handset which serves as a randomizer for combat actions. According to the instructions, the computer "has counters, gates, oscillator clock, programming, read out and execute functions. The 'brain' is made of three miniature TTL logic chips containing over 300 transistors, diodes, and many other components giving a vast computing capacity with a wide range of probabilities for conflict purposes."

In essence, this is a straightforward  hex-map based battle game for up to four players playing individually or as teams. It contains plastic miniatures and a rectangular game board with pre-marked battlefield features. There are no battle cards or other modifiers apart from the handset. The game depends entirely upon the handset to determine the amount of movement and the result of any attack. The handset therefore replaces dice, but serves much the same function. However, players have the freedom to select position, and direction of movement, and the miniatures are marked with values that determine their firing power, and this has a bearing on the result of combat actions (see below).

Each team deploys a regiment of 8 of tanks (slow moving, high fire power) and 4 armoured cars (fast moving, low fire power) in a pre-mapped fictive country on the game board (Kazaldis, Armania, Sarapan and Calderon) separated by marked national borders (which are red) and terrain features such as a river (with bridges), forests, mountains, and buildings. Each country also has a major city marked by a blue border. Inside the blue border several buildings are marked, into which tanks cannot move, meaning they must navigate the city to get to the enemy HQ or get out of their service/repair area and back into combat.

The objective is simple: to win by occupying the opponent or opponents' headquarters by moving a tank (or armoured car) into the HQ space (pre-marked on the board), or by destroying all enemy tanks.

Though the mechanic is therefore a fairly standard hex and counter skirmish, the computer in the handset ultimately drives the game.

The handset consists of six small red bulbs, two buttons and three switches. The lights appear over six icons indicating conditions of movement (the computer determines 'movement points' which may be spent according to terrain features - for example moving through a city costs one movement point per movement but armoured cars moving in open country may move two spaces per movement point), result of firing, repair status and range of attack.

The bulbs have three states - on, off, and flashing, each of which has a different meaning (see below). Of the three switches, one is a power switch. The other two switches determine the conditions of the 'roll' on any given player's turn. One indicates whether the player has opted to attack or retreat (the selection becomes a variable in the computer's calculation of the result), the other on whether the tank is moving or being repaired (tanks under repair cannot move or fire, but if the handset indicates that the service has been successful, the tank can move and fire in that turn).

The handset also has two buttons: one red, one black. The black button is pressed for between 2 and 5 seconds to command the computer to run a computation cycle that will determine movement and repair status (depending on the switches). It will also secretly determine a firing result that will be shown if and when the player presses the 'fire' button (firing is optional).

The red button is for firing. When a player wishes to fire, they press this button and the computer indicates the result of the firing action. If the bulb is fully lit, the result is a hit and the tank with the lower fire power is destroyed. In the case of equal firepower, the attacking tank is considered to have the advantage and destroys the defender. If the bulb flashes, the tank with the lower fire power is damaged and must be placed on a service space for repair. If the bulb stays dark, the attack has failed. According to the instructions "When the computer is programmed for Assault the 'firing result' probability is expressed as a 50% chance of a Hit, 25% chance of Damage and 25% chance of a Miss, while the chance of a 'Service Ready' is 25%. When the computer is programmed for Retreat the 'firing result' probability is expressed as a 25% chance of a Hit, 25% chance of Damage and 50% chance of a Miss, while the chance of 'service ready' is 25%."

Firing is further complicated by the game board and player actions though. A tank in open country can only fire at an enemy tank in a directly adjacent hex in front of or behind it, and it must turn to face in the direction it wishes to fire. Within city boundaries a tank may fire at an enemy in any hex in contact with the one it occupies, though it must again turn to face the target. Ranged firing (two spaces) is possible in open country, but is again a random result from the handset. A tank which is allowed a ranged attack by the handset may fire across lakes and rivers, but not mountains.

Assault Of The Ogroids is a computer moderated solo board game published in the May 1987 issue of Sinclair User magazine, a computer magazine for the 8-bit SInclair ZX Spectrum.

The player takes the part of Deerfoot, a scout sent to gain aid for his city which is being besieged by the evil Ogroids.  The board is a rectangle 12 spaces by 15 spaces, and the player wins the game by traveling across the board within 30 hours.

Every square entered takes a toll of the available time and the player's stamina.  There are five different types of terrain, and the random encounters include finding useful items, meeting unfriendly monsters, becoming lost or finding a quicker route to your eventual goal.  You lose once 30 game hours have passed, or if you are killed by a monster or other kind of encounter.

The computer program is approximately 90 lines of BASIC. It randomises numbers for combat and for encounters met along the way, and also keeps a track of the gold and items held by the player.

Gary Chalk designed the game and also illustrated it in a style familiar from the "Lone Wolf" game books.

Di End is very simple abstract game for 2 players. It could be played everywhere, every time, and could be played with chess or draughts (8x8) set. You've got 8 pawns each, all in 1. (or 8.) row. You must capture maximum possible number of opponents pawns in your turn. To win, you must capture all of opponents pawns or to move all your pawns which still are on the board to the last row. Ties are rare, but possible. You must pay attention, because it's possible to lose more than 5 pawns in one move, even if it doesn't look like. Easy to learn, hard (or maybe impossible) to perfect.

The Quartetto is played on a common 8x8 checkerboard.

Each player has four checkers, each initially placed at the middle four board positions of the opposite rows.

Checkers are moved horizontally or vertically any number of unoccupied board positions. Game play alternates between the two players.

The goal of the game is to place all own checkers in board positions, which middle points are vertices of some rotated square (being non-parallel towards board edges). The smallest surrounding box of this constellation being parallel to edges of the board must be greater or equal 5x5 board positions.

This is a light weight 'filler' strategy game where you explore dungeons, kill things, take their stuff, return to town, heal your party, gain some levels, then start all over again with an even harder dungeon.

It's basically the stereotypical fantasy RPG done up as a strategy game where you manage a party of characters rather than role play a single character.

Fantasy Adventurers is designed to be a light, simple and quick two-player game that can be played almost anywhere, but includes rules for solo play and three players.

The game is only available as a PDF download and includes these PDF files: Adventurers Guide, Game Play Guide, Dungeon Builders Guide, and Character Sheets.

An evolution of the game "Rapid 4", "Rapid 4 More" sees players try to get four marbles in a row by dropping them into holes at the top of a vertical tray much like in Connect Four. The drop, however, is filled with levers that shift as the marble drops through them; players must drop marbles to shift the levers so as to drop marbles into scoring or blocking positions on the slotted gameboard below.

"Rapid 4 More" utilizes two boards instead of one, and introduces an element of negotiation between players -- block a winning opponent, make a move of one's own, or forgo moving altogether in order to block another.

Strike! is a loosely based simulation of the British car industry of the 1970's and was published free in a 1981 edition of Games & Puzzles magazine.

Players are paired together and act as representatives of their respective car manufactures. Each of the pair takes on the role of managing director, the other as union leader of its' work force.

The role of the managing director is one of expanding sales and profits against competition from other managing directors in the rival car firms.

The role of the union leader is to secure fair deal for the workers and secure the highest possible share of the company profits against the resistance of the management.

Strike! asks you to do the impossible: combine with players holding diametrically opposite interests to achieve your objectives within the company while helping to sweep the competition from the market.

description:
Hand-Off combines the strategy and decision making of playing cards with the drama and tension of college football. You take control of the action – bust out a big run, throw a game-breaking long bomb, make the big open-field tackle, or pick off the pass and take it the other way for a touchdown.

Hand-Off is a new and improved version of CSE Games' original critically-acclaimed Card Football: Premiere Edition game.

For one to four players, Hand-Off takes about 45 minutes to play and features the following editions (with more to come):

Hand-Off also features Team Cards highlighting some of the best teams of all-time.

Bragging rights are on the line as players battle through four fast-paced quarters of Hand-Off action.

Bunjee is a simple dice rolling game with some risk management. Each player is playing a figure jumping from a tree, trying to fall low enough to reach the goal but not too low to avoid crashing on the floor. On his turn, the player launch the dies, hecks the result and decides to satisfy himself with the result of the dices or roll some dices again to better his score. Of course, re-rolling the dices includes some risk. Mechanism is very close to the Can't Stop from Sid Sackson.

The aim of the game is to win all 60 chips. Before playing starts the chips are given a certain value. It is allow just to play for the own honor.

From the designer:

In fact, Hazar is some kind of "Las Vegas in your pocket", played in Bars and Cafes, by groups having fun with losing or winning money... There are 60 steel-chips. Up to 12 players (5 chips each) put their chips on numbers in different combinations. The stakes are high, because, in the end, there is only one player, who wins the 60 chips.

20TH and 21ST Century Skirmish Wargame Rules

These miniatures rules have the unique feature of, your side keeps going until you either fail at something, or the other side successfully interrupts your actions.

From the designer notes:
"The rules are designed to be used for World War Two, Korea, Vietnam and The Soviet-Afghan War. That does not mean that they can’t be used for other actions more current or further in the past. I know that some folks use them for World War One, The Spanish Civil War, The Falklands and current actions in Afghanistan and Iraq."

"I think that each player can handle a platoon to a Company. That might mean two platoons of infantry (about thirty to sixty infantry) and a platoon of Afv’s three to five strong. I really believe your table space will dictate how many toys you want to put on the table. A Company per side or less will probably run one to two hours to play."

The basic unit is the battalion, usually comprised of three stands of infantry or three vehicles. The basic command formation would be (depending on nation/year) a Brigade or Division. Each Brigade/Division has an attached commander, and each side has a CinC stand. Commanders have Leadership ratings which influence initiative and the pace of the game. The overall rating of a formation determines which Decision Die (Activation) it uses, which determines how many units may act, how many auto-rallies you get, etc.
Units are rated by morale, which is the key element of the game. A unit's morale determines which attack die you use, which morale die you use, and how many times it can recover from panicking.

Fire Combat: The attacker must make an opposed die roll to hit and damage its target. The attacker rolls a die based upon its morale grade, so a Militia unit roles a d6 while an Elite unit roles a d12 to hit. The defender rolls a Cover Die based upon covering terrain; open (d4), light cover (d6), type of tank (light, Med. Heavy) etc. The difference determines how many disorder markers the target takes, and can force a Panic Check.

Attached companies provide fire support, and special units are important in the game. A unit can have up to four attached companies that provide Fire Die bonuses, help recover Disorder markers, etc. Example, a Battalion with an attached HMG company (+2), Mortar Company (+1), AA Company and Medical Company would get a +3 on its Fire Die, force attacking aircraft to make a Morale Check to press a ground attack, and automatically remove one Disorder Marker per turn.

The rules are fairly comprehensive and cover all the basics of the period -- air superiority over the battlefield, ground attack, artillery, smoke, amphibious landings, fortifications, mines, engineers, supply, paratroopers, gliders, etc.

Also included is a set of rules on generating battles and battlefield terrain, basic army lists, equipment lists, and some sample battles and campaigns.

To find the treasure the dwarves have to use several items like a shovel, a lantern etc. To make the task harder the mine is haunted by a dangerous ghost which can steal the treasure from the dwarves or even force them to start their search from the beginning.

The mechanics are: a modular board changed by the players during play, and various powers the different characters possess.
